title: On the generalization of thermodynamic properties for selection of bioseparation processes
abstract:
Journal of Chemical Technology and Biotechnology
J Chem Technol Biotechnol 74:275–283 (1999)
On the generalization of thermodynamic
properties for selection of bioseparation
processes¹
L A M van der Wielen* and E S J Rudolph
Kluyver Laboratory for Biotechnology, Delft Univers ity of Technology, Julianalaan 67, 2628 BC Delft, The Netherlands
Abstract: Rational selection of multiphase systems as required in bioseparation equipment and
sequences in bioseparation processes beneüts tremendously from the availability of accurate and
reliable thermodynamic data and predictive models. In this paper, we comment on a general metho-
dology to correlate limiting thermodynamic properties which are of use in a wide variety of existing
separation processes, including crystallization, aqueous–organic and aqueous two-phase system
extraction, ion exchange, sorption and membrane processes. It is also demonstrated that this
approach helps in developing quantitative insight into complex heterogeneous systems such as CO2-
extraction with organic solvents. It is shown that the parameters in this general methodology
aided
can be obtained from a limited number of experiments, translated across the boundaries of diþ erent
separation techniques and be predicted from data that are commonly found in the characterization of
the ünal products. In addition, some drawbacks and pittfalls of this approach are addressed.
1999 Society of Chemical Industry
(
Keywords: multiphase systems; bioseparation; protein puriücation; aqueous two-phase systems

INTRODUCTION
Conversions in üne chemicals and biotechnological
processes are seldom complete and absolutely selec-
tive. Even when the desired product is present as the

dominant species, low levels of byproducts and
unconverted precursors cannot be neglected. Selec-
tion of puriücation systems for these structurally
very
related
species
is
troublesome and
is
not
obvious in the case of multi-step separation pro-
cesses. A quantitative comparison of alternatives is
required, which asks in turn for quantiücation of
molecular properties and operating conditions. Intu-
itive
qualitative
concepts,
based
on
substantial
empirical experience have been and are being used to
quantify and rank molecular properties and oper-
ating conditions of processes in order to facilitate the
selection of process alternatives. Clearly, identifying
generalizing quantitative principles in the determi-
nation of thermodynamic properties can assist in
translating this valuable knowledge into quantitative
tools, such as computerized models.
This study is targeted on relatively small bio-
molecules that may carry several charges and can be
overall neutral (zwitterionic species) but also may
have a net charge (ions) such as amino acids, various
b-lactam antibiotics and small peptides. With respect
to the latter the study is restricted to medium-sized
peptides comprising less than approximately eight
amino
acid
residues,
for
which
conformational
changes may not be signiücant. Larger species may
exhibit a phase behaviour that is strongly aþected by
conformational changes, that could be induced by
the environment. This complicates the interpretation
and is the subject of ongoing research. Although the
general thermodynamic framework may, in principle,
be extended to other classes of biomolecules, space
here does not allow such an elaboration.
A starting point for developing a generalizing
methodology, is to recognize common conditions.
One of the obvious observations is the diluted and
usually aqueous environment in which dissolved bio-
products and their contaminants occur. Although the
database with (asymmetric) activity coefficients of
biomolecules is limited to amino acids, it is now clear
that the departures from the inünite dilution stan-
dard state and practically negligible. Selecting a ther-
modynamic framework that recognizes this aspect is
expected to generate reliable descriptions for compa-
rable
systems,
but
also
be
a
sound
basis
for
developing theories that describe phase behaviour in
more dissimilar systems realistically. Secondly, the
deviations of these conditions are expected to be cap-
tured by relatively simple models, with parameters
that are relatively easily transferred to diþerent
systems. In this work, we will derive such a frame-
work and demonstrate its versatility to correlate
phase behaviour of fairly diþerent systems.

Selection of bioseparation processes
coworkers3, this term represents the ‘excess self-
energy’ of the solute-free phase system. This term
reýects the extra energy that is required to disrupt
interactions between solvent molecules to insert a
solute in a ‘cavity’ in the phase system. Positive
values represent unfavourable insertions, and nega-
tive values represent favourable processes. Haynes
and coworkers demonstrated that this term may be
dominating in some aqueous two-phase systems.3
Similar phenomena in other separation systems, are
Solubilities in mixed solvents
Most biomolecules at ambient conditions are solids
when pure. Therefore crystallization is usually one of
the last steps in the puriücation process. In this
work, it is the starting point for the application of the
thermodynamic framework. The solubility of solute s
in a single solvent is described with the following
RT \ ln Hsj ] ln xsjsat ] ln csj*sat

Substitution of eqn (4) into eqn (7), results in the
The unknown chemical potential of the solid phase is
eliminated by substituting eqn (6) in eqn (8), which
leads to the expression below:
For liquid mixtures, that are considered ideal in
this framework, eqn (9) reduces to a simple logarith-
mic average of the solubilities of the solute in each of
the ‘pure’ solvents in the mixture, weighted by their
respective solute-free mole fraction:
For real liquid phase mixtures of biomolecules, it
solubilities are usually very low. Therefore, the
asymmetric activity coefficients of the solute are
expected to be close to unity, as is demonstrated to
be true for amino acids in water.4 In earlier work1,5
an expression was derived for the excess Henry’s
binatorial expression combined with a Margules
residual term. The combinatorial part of this expres-
sion reads for a single solute (s) in a binary solvent

Note that the binary solvent–solvent interaction A12
can be obtained from conventional VLE data from
the literature.6 This leaves the ternary parameter
as the only adjustable parameter. This param-
eter proved to be speciüc for an amino acid and fairly
insensitive to the actual solvent system, in the case of
(water ] two alkanols7) solvent mixtures. Results
from Ref 7, in the form of glycine partition coeffi-
cients, are given in Fig 1. Here, the solvent composi-
tions of the liquid phases were aþected by the
glycine, but the prediction of the partition coefficient
– based on the modiüed solvent compositions –
UNIQUAC-based combinatorial term, replacing the
Flory–Huggins type combinatorial expression may
For pure crystals such as those of amino acids, the
assumption of constant crystal composition may be
valid, but certainly not for biomolecules that form
crystal hydrates in the presence of water. Also in the
case of polymorphism which may occur already with
simple biomolecules such as glycine the derivation is
more involved and not included here.9
Partitioning in mixed solvents and aqueous
Diluted partition coefficients of solutes in multi-
component solvent systems as well as in aqueous
two-phase systems (ATPS) can in principle also be
described by the proposed framework. It should now

In real systems, the reasoning for partition coeffi-
cients of solutes is similar to that for the solubility in
real mixed-solvent systems. The last term of the
above expression usually vanishes because the solutes
are mostly diluted, and their asymmetric activity co-
efficients approach the limiting value of unity at
the excess Henry’s constants of the top and bottom
phases, however, remains. This equation can be
further üne-tuned for particular applications.
A ürst example is the optimization of organic solvent
blends such as used for the extraction of bio-
molecules. When eqn (6) is substituted in eqn (14),
the following result is obtained:

The usefulness of eqn (15) is demonstrated in Fig 2
which shows the mole fraction-based partition coeffi-
cients of a number of amino acids in a partially mis-
cible liquid–liquid system containing 1-butanol and
water. Markers are experimental data from Gude et
al5 and the curves are calculated with eqn (15).
In thermodynamically ideal systems, the logarith-
mic term containing the excess Henry’s constant
vanishes, and the following simple result is obtained:
In the case of extraction systems with aqueous–
mixed organic solvent systems, the solvents are
usually selected such that the solubility of the sol-
vents in the aqueous phase is small. Therefore, it can
be assumed that the composition of the aqueous
phase remains constant and the partition coefficient
of the solute as a function of the solvent composition
can crudely be estimated as follows:

partition coefficients in partially miscible solvents
can be obtained when only the solubilities in each of

of the term from eqn (15) that contains the excess

Selection of bioseparation processes
Henry’s constants may be necessary to improve the
overall description. As a matter of fact, a similar
(combinatorial) correction for eþects of unequal size
of solvent molecules is often used in practice. Then,
the solute-free mole fractions in eqn (17) are replaced
by the corresponding solute-free volume fractions,
and the mole fraction based partition coefficients are
replaced by partition coefficients on a molar basis.
The resulting relationship reads as follows:
Although these corrections are equivalent, eqns
(17) and (18) are not identical. Both eqns (17) and
(18) are simple but powerful tools for optimization of
multi-component organic solvent blends such as
used for the extraction or liquid–liquid chromatog-
raphy of biomolecules. An example of the successful
use of eqn (18) in the development of gradient
separations for liquid–liquid chromatographic tech-
niques was given by Van Buel et al10 They demon-
strated a drastic reduction in laborious experimental
work by using a model which included eqn (18) for
the chromatographic separation of chloromethylated
phenols in heptane/methanol/water systems in a cen-
trifugal partition chromatography (CPC) apparatus.
Some complex gradient patterns were demonstrated
to be optimal for the separation of mixtures of these
components which included combinations of multi-
ple steps and linear gradients in solvent composition.
Van Halsema et al11 used eqn (17) with success to
evaluate a very complex, yet industrially relevant,12
extraction system for carboxylic acids, which are
produced by fermentation. In this extraction system,
an aqueous solution of a salt of a carboxylic acid is
contacted with an organic phase. The organic phase
is composed of a diluent and a reactive extractant
vated pressure. During the extraction process, the
acidiües the aqueous phase, and thus converts
extracted. Depending upon the choice of counterion,
dissolved or precipitated bicarbonates are formed
that can be recycled to control the pH in the fermen-
solvent is crucial since concentrations which are too
Figure 3. Comparis on of meas ured (das hed curves ) and
calculated (s olid curves ) gradient elution of chloromethylated
phenols in heptane/methanol/water s ys tems in a CPC apparatus .
high make the organic phase too apolar for these
acids, and those which are too low do not have suffi-
cient acidifying power. A consistent model in which
eqn (17) plays a key role, could describe the pressure
dependence of lactic acid distribution over organic
organic phase, very well11 as is shown in Fig 4.
Partitioning in other solvent systems
The 1-octanol/water system is only
various biphasic systems for which (diluted) parti-
tition coefficients are available. An overview of the
data available until 1977 was given by Rekker13 who
considered partition coefficients of numerous diluted
solutes in aqueous–organic solvent mixtures with
chloroform, cyclohexane, diethylether, various oils,
n-heptane and iso-butanol. It is tempting to establish
a link between octanol–water partition coefficients
and those in other solvent systems. Collander14 was
the ürst to investigate the relationship between molar
resulted in a relationship of the following general
when the ‘solute (and solvent) sets are not too dissimi-
lar’.13 This relationship can be derived from eqn
(17) (or eqn (18)) by subtracting eqn (17) or (18) for
a solvent system ‘b’ (eg containing iso-butanol) from
this equation for a solvent system ‘o’ containing 1-

lation, the standard deviation increases drastically.15

Correlation of partition coefficients in ATPS
Applications where insight is often blurred by the
occurrence of multiple components are aqueous two-
phase systems. Elaborating the above equation for a
typical ATPS, three ‘solvent’ components should be
incorporated: water (1), a polymer (2) and another,
incompatible polymer or a salt (3). Here, the solu-
bilities of solute s in each of the solvent components
do not exist since the polymers and salts at ambient
are solids when mixed with the solute. To reduce the
expression of the partition coefficient to more simple
terms, we should appreciate the relationship of the
concentrations of the components (polymers, salts) in
the two phases. Haynes and coworkers3 derived the
spinodal curve for a ternary system, which roughly
approximates the binodal curve:
For low concentrations of the polymers and salts
(components 2, 3), this can roughly be interpreted as
a proportionality on mass fraction basis m2 \ k23 m3 .
Therefore, when eqn (14) is used and the excess
Henry’s term as well as the thermodynamic non-
ideality are neglected, this leads to the following
(kj2 ln Hsj)(m2@b [ m2@t)\ ks *m2t@b
system. Clearly, the partition coefficients increase
Figure 5. Po@w of b-lactam antibiotics in octanol/water and
is o-butanol/water. Curve is calculated us ing the Collander14
equation (eqn (19)). Data from Ts uji et al.16
exponentially when the diþerences between the top
and bottom phases in the concentration of a certain
increase linearly. This corresponds to many experi-
mental observations17 (see Fig. 6).
A sensitive and versatile technique to separate struc-
turally similar components is reversed phase liquid
chromatography (RPLC). Although best known for
its analytical applications, the use of RPLC for
process applications in biotechnology is well estab-
lished, for instance as a puriücation step for recom-
binant insulin. In this chromatographic separation
technique, the external and internal surface of a
porous carrier particle is chemically modiüed to be
covered with alkyl chains (typically
mixture to be separated is injected and ýows with a
mobile phase along the stationary phase. Diþerent
partititioning of the various components leads to the
required separation. The degree of partitioning is to
some extent aþected by the exact surface chemistry
groups, but the exact (mixed) solvent composition is
the key operational parameter to üne-tune these
separations. Most researchers agree that the activity
coefficient of the solute in the bonded phase is not
equal to that in the bulk of the solvent. Unfor-
tunately, it is virtually impossible to directly measure
activity coefficients of solutes in the stationary phase.
Cheong and Carr18 stress that ‘the eþects of a change
in the stationary phase composition as sensed by
solutes immersed in the stationary phase are quite
small when compared to the large change in the
solute environment in the mobile phase’. This state-
ment may be interpreted such that the activity coeffi-

cient of the solute in the stationary phase remains
practically constant while continuously varying the
solvent composition from pure solvent 1 to pure
encountered in solid–liquid equilibria, where the
activity coefficient of the solute in the crystal phase
was assumed to be unaþected by the solvent com-
position. Therefore, a similar expression is obtained:
The ürst term on the right hand side of eqn (22)
contains the unknown activity coefficients of the
solute at the sorbent particle. This is the only adjust-
able parameter per solute that characterizes the
stationary phase and is to be obtained from experi-
mental results at one particular solvent combination.

‘Conventional’ excess models are of limited use when
which have a broad variety of interacting polar, non-
polar, H-bonding and charged groups. Nevertheless,
the tendencies in the phase behaviour of these

based on octanol–water partitioning.
In this work, a rigorous model has been devised for
solubilities and partition coefficients of solutes in
single component as well multi-component, partially
miscible solvent mixtures. The model requires the
solubilities of a single solute in each of the pure sol-
vents only and one single parameter to account for
the excess eþects. It is demonstrated that this param-
eter can be correlated to polarity scales, and thereby
to data that are generated in completely diþerent
systems. Furthermore, it was demonstrated that this
approach can be straightforwardly extended to sub-
stantially more complex solvent systems, such as
aqueous two-phase systems and Carbon Dioxide
